item.title: 负载 | Efeito Load
item.description:
马科斯·奥雷里奥

发表于 6 月 16 日

专有animation é usada para animar o elemento，fazendo-o girar continuamente。 不是一般的，esse código CSS combina gradientes de cor e animação para criar um efeito de carregamento visualmente atraente。 一旦暂停，marcosaureliosl 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被解除。

取消暂停后，marcosaureliosl 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，marcosaureliosl 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 marcosaureliosl 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 您可以通过以下方式标记 marcosaureliosl：

降低 marcosaureliosl 发布的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 marcosaureliosl 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Marcos Aurelio

Posted on Jun 16

Esse código CSS é usado para criar um efeito visual de carregamento em uma página da web. Ele define estilos de posicionamento, tamanho e cor para um elemento específico, chamado .load. O elemento é exibido como um quadrado de 80px por 80px.

O efeito de carregamento é criado usando gradientes de cor e transformações. Os gradientes são usados para criar uma transição suave de cores no fundo do elemento, criando um efeito visual interessante. Além disso, são adicionados círculos adicionais ao redor do elemento para enfatizar o efeito de rotação.

A propriedade animation é usada para animar o elemento, fazendo-o girar continuamente. Essa animação é aplicada usando a regra @keyframes e é definida para uma duração de 1 segundo. Isso cria a ilusão de um carregamento em progresso.

No geral, esse código CSS combina gradientes de cor e animação para criar um efeito de carregamento visualmente atraente. Ele pode ser utilizado em sites para indicar que algo está em processo de carregamento ou processamento, proporcionando uma experiência interativa ao usuário.

Once suspended, marcosaureliosl will not be able to comment or publish posts until their suspension is removed.

Once unsuspended, marcosaureliosl will be able to comment and publish posts again.

Once unpublished, all posts by marcosaureliosl will become hidden and only accessible to themselves.

If marcosaureliosl is not suspended, they can still re-publish their posts from their dashboard.

Note:

Thanks for keeping DEV Community safe. Here is what you can do to flag marcosaureliosl:

Make all posts by marcosaureliosl less visible

marcosaureliosl consistently posts content that violates DEV Community's code of conduct because it is harassing, offensive or spammy.

Report other inappropriate conduct

Unflagging marcosaureliosl will restore default visibility to their posts.
item.author: marcosaureliosl

item.title: 如何使用 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试在 Salesforce 中测试电子邮件 | How to Test Emails in Salesforce with Mailtrap Email Testing
item.description:
虽然 Salesforce 的测试功能足以运行小型可传递性测试或预览您的电子邮件，但它们可能不足以详细检查和调试电子邮件。 另外，某些方法可能会冒用测试电子邮件向联系人发送垃圾邮件的风险。

这就是 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试发挥作用的地方。 今天，我们将教您如何使用 Mailtrap 在 Salesforce 中执行各种测试。

什么是 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试？

它捕获所有 SMTP 流量并将您的测试电子邮件捕获在虚拟收件箱中。 电子邮件测试是Mailtrap 电子邮件传送平台的一部分。

电子邮件测试的一些功能包括

  • 一个假的 SMTP 服务器，以消除垃圾邮件用户的风险；
  • 流行垃圾邮件过滤器和黑名单的垃圾邮件分析；
  • 易于集成的 SMTP 和 API；
  • 流行语言和框架的现成代码片段；
  • 针对不同项目和阶段的多个收件箱。

什么时候应该使用 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试？

在几个用例中，您应该选择 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试。 让我们在下面快速讨论它们中的每一个。

避免使用测试电子邮件向用户发送垃圾邮件

通过使用 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试，您可以轻松避免惹恼收件人。 它会在 Salesforce 和您的联系人之间制造某种障碍。 因此，您所有的测试电子邮件最终都会进入一个虚拟收件箱。

测试电子邮件格式和内容

通过电子邮件测试，您可以轻松检查模板中针对不同电子邮件客户端的 HTML/CSS。 您会看到一个详细的图表，其中有问题的代码行被标记为红色。 每个有问题的元素都有对每个客户端的支持级别的描述。

最重要的是，电子邮件测试具有垃圾邮件分析功能。

该功能对于您的送达能力和发件人声誉尤为重要。 为什么？ 如果邮箱提供商可能会自动将您的电子邮件模板标记为垃圾邮件，该分数会清楚地指示您。 此外，您还可以查看您的域或 IP 是否出现在任何黑名单中。

这样，您将有机会在暂存阶段减少电子邮件的垃圾邮件，并在生产环境中保护您的发件人声誉。

将所有测试电子邮件保存在一个地方

电子邮件测试捕获您所有外发的 Salesforce 电子邮件，并将它们组织在不同的收件箱中，用于不同的项目和测试阶段。

因此，电子邮件测试和 Salesforce 为深入测试和调试创建了一个强大的组合。

如何测试电子邮件的送达率

对于下面的说明，我们假设您已经启动并运行了 Salesforce 帐户。

但要开始测试，您还需要设置一个 Mailtrap 帐户（如果您已经拥有，则可以跳过此步骤）。

  1. 转到电子邮件测试 → 收件箱以查找默认创建的第一个收件箱。 它称为“我的收件箱”，但您可以通过单击右侧的铅笔图标轻松重命名它。 如果需要，还可以创建其他项目和收件箱。

  1. 单击您的收件箱并点击显示凭据以显示虚假 SMTP 服务器的凭据。

  1. 转到您的 Salesforce 帐户并导航到设置 → 电子邮件（在管理下）→ 电子邮件中继。

  1. 单击创建电子邮件中继并使用电子邮件测试的 SMTP 凭据填写表单，如下例所示：

  1. 单击启用 SMTP 身份验证以连接到具有身份验证的 SMTP 服务器。 输入您的 SMTP 用户名和密码。

  1. 单击保存。 前往电子邮件域过滤器并单击创建电子邮件域过滤器。 此步骤是激活继电器所必需的。

  1. 在那里，输入“*”作为发件人域和收件人域的默认通配符。 然后标记 Active 复选框，否则将不会应用继电器。 单击保存。

现在是时候用 Apex 发送测试电子邮件，看看它们是否会被发送到电子邮件测试的虚拟收件箱。 有关使用 Apex 发送电子邮件的更多详细信息，请查看此博客文章。

在您的 Salesforce 帐户中，导航至设置并在快速查找框中键入 Apex 类。

单击新建以创建将发送简单电子邮件的新 Apex 类。 粘贴以下代码片段并按保存。

不要忘记用实际的电子邮件地址替换recipient@example.com和no-reply@example.com 。

 public class SendSingleEmail { public void sendSingleEmailMethod(){ Messaging.reserveSingleEmailCapacity(2); Messaging.SingleEmailMessage mymail = new Messaging.SingleEmailMessage(); // Ensure that addresses are correctly formatted and matching case String[] toAddresses = new String[] {'recipient@example.com'}; mymail.setToAddresses(toAddresses); mymail.setReplyTo('no-reply@example.com'); mymail.setSenderDisplayName('Salesforce Support'); mymail.setSubject('Test Email '); mymail.setPlainTextBody('This is the first test email sent from Salesforce with Mailtrap Email Testing'); mymail.setHtmlBody('<p>This is the first test email sent from Salesforce with Mailtrap Email Testing</p>'); Messaging.sendEmail(new Messaging.SingleEmailMessage[] { mymail }); } }

返回 Apex 类页面，单击 Developer Console，然后打开我们刚刚创建的类。 在调试下，选择打开执行匿名窗口。

键入以下代码行并单击执行。

 sendSingleEmail obj = new sendSingleEmail(); obj.sendSingleEmailMethod();

在日志下，将出现一条成功消息。 前往您的 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试收件箱，您会在那里看到测试电子邮件。 这证实了 Salesforce 的交付能力没有任何问题。

如何测试电子邮件模板

在 Salesforce 中测试电子邮件模板非常容易。 由于我们已经配置了电子邮件中继，因此我们只需要执行几个额外的步骤。 为了使我们的测试有趣并利用电子邮件测试的全部功能，让我们分别发送文本和 HTML 模板。

测试文本模板

我们将在 Salesforce 中创建一个示例文本模板。

  1. 转到设置 → 电子邮件 → 经典电子邮件模板，然后单击新建模板。

  1. 选择文本作为模板类型，然后按下一步。

  1. 配置所需的合并字段。 对于此示例，我们选择 Contact Fields 作为字段类型，并选择 First Name 作为字段。 然后，选择将保存模板的文件夹，选中可供使用旁边的复选框，为模板命名，然后输入主题行和正文。 不要忘记插入自动生成的合并字段。 保存模板。

  1. 单击发送测试和验证合并字段。

  1. 在弹出窗口中，选择要为其测试模板的联系人。 勾选发送电子邮件预览至并输入您的电子邮件地址。

  1. 单击确定。 现在检查您的电子邮件测试收件箱。 测试模板就在那里。 但是，您不会在真实的收件箱中找到它，因为我们通过虚假的 SMTP 服务器发送了它。

测试 HTML 模板

就像在前面的示例中一样，我们应该从创建模板开始，但我们选择自定义（不使用经典信头）而不是文本。 点击下一步。

选择文件夹，使模板可供使用，为其指定一个唯一的名称，然后设置主题。 根据 HTML 规范，编码应设置为 Unicode (UTF-8)。

在下一步中，我们可以添加一个 HTML 正文。 这次我们将跳过合并字段并专注于测试 HTML 内容。

然后我们需要配置邮件的文本版本。 这样，即使电子邮件客户端未正确呈现 HTML，电子邮件的正文文本仍然可见。 单击保存。

您将看到电子邮件的预览。 让我们像以前一样发送它，并在我们的虚拟收件箱中检查它。

  1. 首先检查不同设备中的电子邮件预览。 您可以检查手机、平板电脑和桌面视图。

  1. 然后让我们转到 HTML Source 选项卡。 在那里，您会看到所有有问题的元素都标有红色感叹号图标。

  1. 转到 HTML 检查选项卡以更详细地检查客户端支持。 在这里，您可以按特定客户端/设备过滤结果或查看总体 HTML 支持百分比。 查看我们的知识库文章，了解 HTML 检查的工作原理以及如何解释数据。

分析 HTML 检查结果后，您可以调整代码并更正/删除可能阻止 Salesforce 电子邮件在联系人收件箱中呈现的元素。

如何测试电子邮件警报

使用 Mailtrap，您可以测试任何类型的 Salesforce 电子邮件警报。 以下部分详细介绍了所有步骤，但我们将首先创建一个警报。

  1. 搜索电子邮件警报，然后单击“新建电子邮件警报”按钮。

  1. 根据您的测试需求填写字段，然后单击“保存”按钮。 请记住，标有红色的字段是必填字段。 此外，我们使用了示例对象和电子邮件模板，因此您可能需要根据您的测试需要进行更改。

测试电子邮件警报

可以通过工作流规则、进程构建器和流发送电子邮件警报。

以下是每种方法的步骤。

创建一个简单的工作流规则

  1. 导航到工作流规则并单击“新建规则”按钮。

  1. 选择联系人对象。

  1. 在 Evaluation Criteria 下，选择“created, and every time it's aded”。 然后，在规则标准中，在字段下选择联系人电子邮件、运算符“不等于”和“空”值。 最后，单击“保存并下一步”按钮。

  1. 然后，单击“添加工作流操作”按钮并选择现有操作。

  1. 选择您之前创建的电子邮件警报，然后单击“保存”按钮。

  1. 再次转到工作流规则并激活流程。

测试工作流规则电子邮件警报

  1. 转到任何联系人记录并编辑任何字段。 创建或编辑联系人记录时，工作流规则应发送电子邮件警报，然后单击“保存”按钮。

  1. 转到 Mailtrap 并确保电子邮件已送达。

创建一个简单的进程构建器

  1. 导航到 Process Builder，然后单击新建。

  1. 根据您的测试需要填写字段，然后单击“保存”按钮。 提示：使流程和 API 名称与描述一致，以便于参考。 将变量保留在“A 记录更改”的“流程开始时间”下。

  1. 选择 Contact 对象，在“Start the process”字段中选择“only when a record is created”选项，然后单击“Save”按钮。

  1. 根据您的测试需求添加标准——我们根据本文中使用的示例构建了流程。

  1. 单击 IMMEDIATE_ACTIONS 磁贴，在“操作类型”下选择电子邮件警报，然后选择在前面的步骤中创建的电子邮件警报，然后单击“保存”按钮。

  1. 激活流程构建器。

测试 Process Builder 电子邮件警报

提示：如果您只想从 Process Builder 接收电子邮件，请在创建新联系人之前停用工作流规则。 否则，也会执行 Workflow 规则，因为该规则也符合条件。

  1. 转到“联系人”选项卡并创建一个新联系人。

  1. 转到 Mailtrap 并确保电子邮件已送达。

创建一个简单的流程

  1. 导航到流并单击“新建流”按钮。

  1. 选择 Record-Triggered Flow 并单击“Create”按钮。

  1. 根据您的测试需要配置开始，然后单击“完成”按钮。 请注意，您应该将“配置触发器”选项保留为“创建或更新记录”。

  1. 单击白点以添加新元素。

  1. 在快捷方式下选择“发送电子邮件警报”。

  1. 填写要标记的字段并描述操作，然后点击“完成”按钮。

测试流电子邮件警报

提示：如果您只想从 Flow 接收电子邮件，请在创建新联系人之前停用 Workflow 规则和 Process Builder。 否则，也会执行 Workflow 规则和 Process Builder，因为这些规则也符合条件。

  1. 转到“联系人”选项卡并创建一个新联系人。

  1. 转到 Mailtrap 并确保电子邮件已送达。

如何测试批量电子邮件处理

借助 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试，您可以测试 Salesforce 中的批量电子邮件处理。 此测试将验证 Apex 限制并检查您的批量电子邮件是否已正确发送。

与之前的测试一样，Email Testing 的 SMTP 凭据应设置为电子邮件中继。

第一步是通过使用数据导入向导导入数据或手动添加几个示例联系人来创建足够数量的联系人数据。 对于后者，按下应用程序图标并选择营销。

在“联系人”选项卡下，单击“新建”。 输入联系人的姓名，创建一个与联系人关联的新帐户，然后输入电子邮件地址。 保存设置并为您要添加的任何其他联系人重复该过程。

完成后，您的联系人列表应如下所示：

接下来，创建一个示例模板，将其打开并记下模板的 ID。 它位于 URL 中并以 00X 开头。

此时，我们可以开始编写 Apex 类。

使用以下脚本在 Apex 类中添加一个新类：

 public class BulkEmailHandler { public static void sendBulkEmail(List<Id> contactIds) { Messaging.reserveMassEmailCapacity(contactIds.size()); Messaging.MassEmailMessage mail = new Messaging.MassEmailMessage(); mail.setTargetObjectIds(contactIds); mail.setTemplateId('your_template_id'); // replace 'your_template_id' with the actual ID of your email template mail.setSaveAsActivity(false); Messaging.sendEmail(new Messaging.MassEmailMessage[] { mail }); } }

这段代码需要联系人 ID。 因此，要运行脚本，我们应该让 Apex 先获取联系人 ID，然后发送电子邮件。 打开开发者控制台，在 Open Execute Anonymous Window 中，添加以下行：

 List<Contact> contacts = [SELECT Id FROM Contact LIMIT 10]; List<Id> contactIds = new List<Id>(); for (Contact c : contacts) { contactIds.add(c.Id); } BulkEmailHandler.sendBulkEmail(contactIds);

按执行。 几秒钟后，电子邮件将在您的 Mailtrap 虚拟收件箱中弹出，表明您的 Salesforce 平台可以成功发送批量电子邮件。

注意：上面的代码片段默认会向 50 个联系人发送电子邮件。 如果您需要发送更多，您应该更新您的 SOQL 查询。 此外，根据您的 Salesforce 组织类型和您使用的包，您每天的电子邮件发送量也可能会受到限制。

包起来

我们关于使用 Mailtrap 电子邮件测试在 Salesforce 中测试电子邮件的教程到此结束。 现在您可以成功地检查和调试电子邮件的可传递性、文本和 HTML 模板以及批量电子邮件处理。

不要忘记通过创建不同的项目和收件箱来组织您的虚拟收件箱。 这将简化测试过程并将您的数据保存在一个地方。

要了解有关 Mailtrap 的更多信息，请单击此处。

感谢您阅读我们关于在 Salesforce 中使用 Mailtrap 测试电子邮件警报的指南！ 请随时查看 Mailtrap 博客上的原始文章。 ;) ;)



原文:

While Salesforce’s testing capabilities are sufficient for running small deliverability tests or previewing your emails, they may not be enough for inspecting and debugging emails in detail. Plus, some methods may bear the risk of spamming contacts with test emails.

That’s where Mailtrap Email Testing comes into play. And today, we’ll teach you how to execute various tests in Salesforce using Mailtrap.

What is Mailtrap Email Testing?

Mailtrap Email Testing is an Email Sandbox – a safe environment to inspect and debug emails in staging and development environments. It captures all the SMTP traffic and traps your test emails in a virtual inbox. Email Testing is part of the Mailtrap Email Delivery Platform.

Some of the features of Email Testing include

  • A fake SMTP server to eliminate the risk of spamming users;
  • HTML analysis to check how your emails will render in different email clients;
  • Spam analysis for popular spam filters and blacklists;
  • SMTP and API for easy integration;
  • Ready-made code snippets for popular languages and frameworks;
  • Multiple inboxes for different projects and stages.

When should you use Mailtrap Email Testing?

There are several use cases where you should opt for Mailtrap Email Testing. Let’s quickly discuss each of them below.

Avoiding spamming users with test emails

You can easily avoid annoying your recipients by using Mailtrap Email Testing. It will create sort of a barrier between Salesforce and your contacts. As a result, all your test emails will end up in a virtual inbox.

Testing email format and content

With Email Testing, you can easily check HTML/CSS in your templates for different email clients. You’ll see a detailed chart with problematic lines of code marked in red. Each problematic element has a description of the support level for each client.

On top of that, Email Testing has a Spam Analysis feature.

The feature is particularly important for your deliverability and sender reputation. Why? The score gives you a clear indication if mailbox providers may automatically label your email template as spam. Plus, you get to see if your domain or IP appear in any of the blacklists.

That way, you’ll get the chance to make the emails less spammy in staging and protect your sender reputation in production.

Keeping all test emails in one place

Email Testing captures all your outgoing Salesforce emails and keeps them organized in different inboxes for various projects and testing stages.

So, Email Testing and Salesforce create a robust combo for in-depth testing and debugging.

How to test email deliverability

For the instructions below, we’ll assume that you already have a Salesforce account up and running.

But to start testing, you’ll also need to set up a Mailtrap account (if you have it already, you can skip this step).

  1. Go to Email Testing → Inboxes to find your first inbox created by default. It’s called ‘My Inbox’ but you can easily rename it by clicking the pencil icon on the right. It’s also possible to create additional projects and inboxes if needed.

  1. Click on your inbox and hit Show Credentials to reveal the credentials for the fake SMTP server.

  1. Go to your Salesforce account and navigate to Setup → Email (under Administration) → Email Relays.

  1. Click Create Email Relay and fill out the form with Email Testing’s SMTP credentials as in the example below:

  1. Click Enable SMTP Auth to connect to the SMTP server with authentication. Enter your SMTP username and password.

  1. Click Save. Head to Email Domain Filters and click Create Email Domain Filter. This step is necessary to activate the relay.

  1. There, enter ‘*’ as the default wildcard for Sender Domain and Recipient Domain. Then mark the Active checkbox, otherwise the relay won’t be applied. Click Save.

Now it’s time to send test emails with Apex and see if they will be delivered to the Email Testing’s virtual inbox. For more details on sending emails with Apex, check out this blog post.

In your Salesforce account, navigate to Setup and type Apex Classes in a quick find box.

Click New to create a new Apex class that will send a simple email. Paste the following snippet and press Save.

Don’t forget to substitute recipient@example.com and no-reply@example.com with actual email addresses.

public class SendSingleEmail {
    public void sendSingleEmailMethod(){

        Messaging.reserveSingleEmailCapacity(2);
        Messaging.SingleEmailMessage mymail = new Messaging.SingleEmailMessage();

        // Ensure that addresses are correctly formatted and matching case
        String[] toAddresses = new String[] {'recipient@example.com'};

        mymail.setToAddresses(toAddresses);
        mymail.setReplyTo('no-reply@example.com');
        mymail.setSenderDisplayName('Salesforce Support');
        mymail.setSubject('Test Email ');
        mymail.setPlainTextBody('This is the first test email sent from Salesforce with Mailtrap Email Testing');
        mymail.setHtmlBody('<p>This is the first test email sent from Salesforce with Mailtrap Email Testing</p>');

        Messaging.sendEmail(new Messaging.SingleEmailMessage[] { mymail });
    }
}

Go back to the Apex classes page, click Developer Console, and open the class we just created. Under debug, choose Open Execute Anonymous Window.

Type the following line of code and click Execute.

sendSingleEmail obj = new sendSingleEmail();
obj.sendSingleEmailMethod();

Under logs, a success message will appear. Head to your Mailtrap Email Testing inbox and you’ll see the test email there. This confirms that Salesforce deliverability doesn’t have any issues.

How to test email templates

Testing email templates is quite easy in Salesforce. And since we already have email relay configured, we just need to take a couple of additional steps. To make our tests interesting and leverage the full capabilities of Email Testing, let’s send text and HTML templates separately.

Testing text templates

We’ll create a sample text template in Salesforce.

  1. Go to Setup → Email → Classic Email Templates and click New Template.

  1. Select Text as the template type and press Next.

  1. Configure the desired merged fields. For this example, we chose Contact Fields as the field type and the First Name as the field. Then, choose the folder in which the template will be saved, mark the checkbox next to Available For Use, name the template, and enter the subject line and body text. Don’t forget to insert the auto-generated merge field. Save the template.

  1. Click Send Test and Verify Merge Fields.

  1. In the pop-up window, choose the contact you want to test the template for. Tick Send email preview to and enter your email address.

  1. Click OK. Now check your Email Testing inbox. The test template will be right there. However, you won’t find it in your real inbox since we sent it through the fake SMTP server.

Testing HTML templates

Just like in the previous example, we should start by creating a template, but instead of text, let’s select Custom (without using Classic Letterhead). Click Next.

Select the folder, make the template available for use, give it a unique name, and set the subject. The Encoding should be set to Unicode (UTF-8) as per HTML specifications.

In the next step, we can add an HTML body. This time we’ll skip the merge field and concentrate on testing the HTML content.

Then we need to configure the text version of the email. That way, the body text of the email will still be visible even if the email client doesn’t render the HTML properly. Click Save.

You will see the preview of the email. Let’s send it as we did before and inspect it in our virtual inbox.

  1. Start by checking the email preview in different devices. You can check mobile, tablet, and desktop views.

  1. Then let’s move to the HTML Source tab. There, you’ll see all the problematic elements marked with a red exclamation icon.

  1. Go to the HTML check tab to inspect client support in more detail. Here, you can filter results by a specific client/device or view the overall HTML support percentage. Check out our knowledge base article to learn how HTML check works and how to interpret data.

After analyzing the HTML check results, you can adjust the code and correct/remove elements that might stop your Salesforce emails from rendering in your contacts’ inboxes.

How to test email alerts

With Mailtrap, you can test any type of Salesforce email alert. The following sections detail all the steps, but we’ll start by creating an alert first.

  1. Search for Email Alerts, then click “New Email Alert” button.

  1. Fill out the fields based on the your testing needs and click the “Save” button. Keep in mind that the fields labeled red are mandatory. Also, we used exemplary object and email template, so you may need to change that based on your testing needs.

Testing Email Alerts

An Email Alert can be sent via Workflow Rules, Process Builder, and the Flow.

Here are the steps for each method.

Create a simple Workflow rule

  1. Navigate to the Workflow Rules and click the “New Rule” button.

  1. Select the Contact object.

  1. Under Evaluation Criteria, choose “created, and every time it’s aded”. Then, at Rule Criteria choose Contact Email under Fields, Operator “not equal to”, and the “null” Value. Lastly, click the Save&Next button.

  1. Thereon, click the “Add Workflow Action” button and select Existing Action.

  1. Choose the Email Alert which you previously created and click the ”Save” button.

  1. Go to the Workflow Rules one more time and activate the process.

Test the Workflow Rule Email Alert

  1. Go to any contact record and edit any field. The Workflow rule should send an Email Alert when a contact record is created or edited, then thr click “Save” button.

  1. Go to Mailtrap and make sure that the email is delivered.

Create a simple Process Builder

  1. Navigate to the Process Builder, then click New.

  1. Fill out the fields based on your testing needs, and click the “Save” button. Tip: Make the Process and API name and the Description congruent for easier reference. Keep the variable under “The process starts when” at “A record changes”.

  1. Choose the Contact object and in “Start the process” field select “only when a record is created” option, then click the “Save” button.

  1. Add Criteria based on your testing needs – we built the Process based on the example used in this article.

  1. Click the IMMEDIATE_ACTIONS tile, and under the “Action Type” choose Email Alerts, then select the Email Alert created in the previous steps, and click the “Save” button.

  1. Activate Process builder.

Test the Process Builder Email Alerts

Tip: If you want to get email only from Process Builder, deactivate Workflow rules before creating a new contact. Otherwise, will be executed Workflow rule too because this rule also meet the criteria.

  1. Go to the Contact tab and create a new contact.

  1. Go to Mailtrap and make sure that the email is delivered.

Create a simple flow

  1. Navigate to Flows and click the “New flow” button.

  1. Select Record-Triggered Flow and click the “Create” button.

  1. Configure Start as per your testing needs and click the “Done” button. Note that you should keep the Configure Trigger option at “A record is created or updated”.

  1. Click the white dot to add a new element.

  1. Under Shortcuts choose “Sent Email Alert”.

  1. Fill out the fields to label and describe the action, then hit the “Done” button.

Test the Flow Email Alert

Tip: If you want to get email only from Flow, deactivate Workflow rules and Process Builder before creating a new contact. Otherwise, will be executed Workflow rule and Process Builder too because these rule also meet the criteria.

  1. Go to the Contact tab and create a new contact.

  1. Go to Mailtrap and make sure that the email is delivered.

How to test bulk email handling

With the help of Mailtrap Email Testing, you can test bulk email handling in Salesforce. This test will validate the Apex limits and check if your bulk emails are getting delivered properly.

Just like with previous tests, Email Testing’s SMTP credentials should be set up as an email relay.

The first step is to create a sufficient amount of contact data by importing data with a Data Import Wizard or adding a couple of sample contacts manually. For the latter, press on the application icon and choose Marketing.

Under the Contacts tab, click New. Enter the name of the contact, create a new account to associate with the contact, and enter the email address. Save the settings and repeat the process for any other contact you want to add.

Once you’re done, your contacts list should look something like this:

Next, create a sample template, open it and note the template’s ID. It’s located in the URL and starts with 00X.

At this point, we can start writing an Apex Class.

Add a new class in Apex classes with the following script:

public class BulkEmailHandler {
    public static void sendBulkEmail(List<Id> contactIds) {
        Messaging.reserveMassEmailCapacity(contactIds.size());

        Messaging.MassEmailMessage mail = new Messaging.MassEmailMessage();

        mail.setTargetObjectIds(contactIds);
        mail.setTemplateId('your_template_id'); // replace 'your_template_id' with the actual ID of your email template
        mail.setSaveAsActivity(false);

        Messaging.sendEmail(new Messaging.MassEmailMessage[] { mail });
    }
}

This piece of code requires contact IDs. So, to run the script, we should ask Apex to grab contact IDs first and then send an email. Open the developer console and in the Open Execute Anonymous Window, add the following lines:

List<Contact> contacts = [SELECT Id FROM Contact LIMIT 10];
List<Id> contactIds = new List<Id>();
for (Contact c : contacts) {
    contactIds.add(c.Id);
}
BulkEmailHandler.sendBulkEmail(contactIds);

Press Execute. In a few seconds, the emails will pop up in your Mailtrap virtual inbox indicating that your Salesforce platform can successfully send bulk emails.

Note: The code piece above will send an email to 50 contacts by default. You should update your SOQL query if you need to send more. Also, depending on the type of your Salesforce org and the package you’re using, your daily email sending volume may also be limited.

Wrapping up

This wraps up our tutorial on testing emails in Salesforce with Mailtrap Email Testing. Now you can successfully inspect and debug email deliverability, text and HTML templates, and bulk email handling.

Don’t forget to organize your virtual inbox by creating different projects and inboxes. That will simplify the testing process and keep your data in one place.

To learn more about Mailtrap, click here.

Thanks for reading our guide on testing email alerts with Mailtrap in Salesforce! Feel free to check out the original piece on the Mailtrap Blog. ;)
item.author: sofiatarhonska

item.title: 使用 Three.js 从头开始​​制作 Resend 立方体 | Making the Resend cube from scratch using Three.js
item.description:
彼得波哥

发布于 6 月 16 日 • 最初发布于petttr1.github.io

我的大脑立即运转起来，试图弄清楚他们是怎么做到的。

由于我对 Three.js 有一些经验，所以我直接进去了。

从样板文件开始

这为您设置了最基本的场景 - 一个白色立方体、黑色背景和立方体上的标准材质，这意味着您应该看到阴影和其他东西。 很简约。 不客气。 让立方体变得更小（更圆）

事实证明，三个js并没有直接解决这个问题。 所以一个快速的谷歌搜索产生了这个简洁的小功能（来源）

 function createBoxWithRoundedEdges( width, height, depth, radius0, smoothness ) { let shape = new THREE.Shape(); let eps = 0.00001; let radius = radius0 - eps; shape.absarc( eps, eps, eps, -Math.PI / 2, -Math.PI, true ); shape.absarc( eps, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI, Math.PI / 2, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI / 2, 0, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, eps, eps, 0, -Math.PI / 2, true ); let geometry = new THREE.ExtrudeBufferGeometry( shape, { amount: depth - radius0 * 2, bevelEnabled: true, bevelSegments: smoothness * 2, steps: 1, bevelSize: radius, bevelThickness: radius0, curveSegments: smoothness }); geometry.center(); return geometry; }

调用该函数会生成一个带有圆角的整洁小立方体。 您可以使用第 4 个参数 - radius0 调整您希望圆角的程度。 材料+灯

现在我们希望立方体感觉像重新发送的立方体 - 这意味着我们需要将其制成金属和不同的光。 金属很简单——我们已经使用了标准材料，所以我们只需添加一些金属感并降低粗糙度（当我们这样做时，我们将颜色更改为深灰色）。

 const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x2a2a2a, metalness: 1, roughness: 0.11 });

现在立方体褪色了很多——它几乎融入了背景，你偶尔会看到一些闪光。 我们需要用点光源替换灯光并调整它以始终照亮立方体的某些部分。

与沿特定方向发射的定向光相比（但非常微弱并且落在立方体的一侧会产生“手电筒”效果 - 您会看到受到影响的小表面），点光向各个方向发射光，从而产生我们可以使用一个很好的效果 - 立方体中反射的光会更大，并且总是照射到表面 - 边缘以获得更酷的效果。 我们创建了一个相当大的点光源，能够照亮立方体的整个侧面。

我们还可以为底色添加第二盏灯。 将它放在立方体下方可确保我们始终突出显示某些部分。

 // Create the light const light = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 100, 500 ); light.position.set( 0, 10, 10 ); scene.add( light ); const light2 = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 10, 500 ); light2.position.set( 0, -10, -5 ); scene.add( light2 );

您可以使用材料属性和灯光（强度、颜色）来创建一些很酷的效果。

制作婴儿立方体

有趣的部分来了。 我们将创建另一个函数，它将在一些嵌套循环中调用圆边立方体函数并制作 27 个立方体。 我们走吧。

 function makeCubes() { const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x1a1a1a, metalness: 1, roughness: 0.18 }); const numCubes = 3; // Create the group, we will add cubes to the group const cubes = new THREE.Group(); // iterate over all dimensions + trick to center the cube for (let i = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i++) { for (let j = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j++) { for (let k = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k++) { // adding the cubes const geom = createBoxWithRoundedEdges(1, 1, 1, .1, 20); geom.translate(i, j, k); const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geom, material); cubes.add(cube); } } } return cubes; }

我们现在需要做的就是调用它：

 // Create a cube const cube = makeCubes(); scene.add(cube);

添加控件

作为奖励，我们可以添加控件并根据需要旋转立方体。 如果您想禁用自动旋转，只需注释掉两条cube.rotation行。

首先我们需要导入插件脚本。 将此行放在三个导入脚本的下方。

 <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three-addons@1.2.0/build/three-addons.min.js"></script>

然后我们添加控件 - 您可以将其添加到任何地方，例如靠近 camera = ... 行。

 const controls = new THREE_ADDONS.OrbitControls(camera,renderer.domElement);

最后我们编辑每次渲染时调用的动画函数。

 // Animate the cube function animate() { requestAnimationFrame(animate); cube.rotation.x += 0.005; cube.rotation.y -= 0.005; controls.update(); renderer.render(scene, camera); }

为什么重新发送的立方体看起来更好？

  1. 在三个 js 中添加（ example_1 ， example_2 ）过于复杂，我太懒了。 我实际上已经在另一个项目中实现过一次，我可能会在其他时间分享。
  2. 迷你立方体不一样——重新发送的“正方形”具有不同的几何形状，“正方形”也被挤压成具有圆角的立方体。 尝试腾出一些时间对你和我来说可能是一个很好的锻炼。
  3. 旋转层的效果——这看起来很酷，理论上可以通过只旋转一层来实现——就在旋转之前，你可以通过一个预先定义的随机变换来翻转立方体，在其中一侧旋转它。 这样您就可以创建始终旋转另一侧的效果。
  4. 更好的闪电 - 钉住闪电很困难，所以我尽可能地接近（我给了它 2 分钟）。

我鼓励您添加这些功能并在结果中标记我 - 也许在Twitter上。 玩得开心。

最终代码：

 <!DOCTYPE html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>Resend Cube</title> <style> body { margin: 0; } canvas { display: block; } </style> </head> <body> <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.142.0/build/three.min.js"></script> <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three-addons@1.2.0/build/three-addons.min.js"></script> <script type="module"> function createBoxWithRoundedEdges( width, height, depth, radius0, smoothness ) { let shape = new THREE.Shape(); let eps = 0.00001; let radius = radius0 - eps; shape.absarc( eps, eps, eps, -Math.PI / 2, -Math.PI, true ); shape.absarc( eps, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI, Math.PI / 2, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI / 2, 0, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, eps, eps, 0, -Math.PI / 2, true ); let geometry = new THREE.ExtrudeBufferGeometry( shape, { depth: depth - radius0 * 2, bevelEnabled: true, bevelSegments: smoothness * 2, steps: 1, bevelSize: radius, bevelThickness: radius0, curveSegments: smoothness }); geometry.center(); return geometry; } function makeCubes() { const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x2a2a2a, metalness: 1, roughness: 0.11 }); const numCubes = 3; // Create the group, we will add cubes to the group const cubes = new THREE.Group(); // iterate over all dimensions for (let i = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i++) { for (let j = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j++) { for (let k = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k++) { // adding the cubes const geom = createBoxWithRoundedEdges(1, 1, 1, .17, 20); geom.translate(i, j, k); const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geom, material); cubes.add(cube); } } } return cubes; } // Create the scene const scene = new THREE.Scene(); scene.background = new THREE.Color( 0x000000 ); // Create the light const light = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 100, 500 ); light.position.set( 0, 5, 10 ); scene.add( light ); const light2 = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 10, 500 ); light2.position.set( 0, -10, -5 ); scene.add( light2 ); // Create the camera const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(75, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 1000); // Create the renderer const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer(); renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight); document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement); // Create a cube const cube = makeCubes(); scene.add(cube); // Position the camera camera.position.z = 10; const controls = new THREE_ADDONS.OrbitControls(camera,renderer.domElement); // Animate the cube function animate() { requestAnimationFrame(animate); cube.rotation.x += 0.005; cube.rotation.y -= 0.005; controls.update(); renderer.render(scene, camera); } animate(); </script> </body> </html>

一旦暂停，sal_paradise 将无法发表评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被删除。

解除暂停后，sal_paradise 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

一旦取消发布，sal_paradise 的所有帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果 sal_paradise 没有被暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 以下是标记 sal_paradise 的方法：

降低 sal_paradise 的所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

取消标记 sal_paradise 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Peter Bokor

Posted on Jun 16 • Originally published at petttr1.github.io

4 months ago - on 30th January, I have seen the resend.com webpage with the amazing looking 3D cube. My brain immediately kicked into gear trying to figure out how they did it.

4 months later, their recent launch reminded me of this desire of mine to steal it (Steal like an artist reference, yay!). As I have some experience with Three.js, I went right in.

Start with a boilerplate

This sets you up with the most basic scene - a single white cube, black background, and standard material on the cube, meaning you should see the shadows and stuff. Pretty neat. You're welcome. (I have actually stolen this and adapted it a bit from 3dcodekits; thanks 3dcodekits, I love you!).

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>Resend Cube</title> <style> body { margin: 0; } canvas { display: block; } </style>
</head>
<body>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.142.0/build/three.min.js"></script>
<script> // Create the scene const scene = new THREE.Scene(); scene.background = new THREE.Color( 0x000000 ); // Create the light const light = new THREE.DirectionalLight( 0xffffff ); light.position.set( 0, 0.5, 1 ); scene.add( light ); // Create the camera const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(75, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 1000); // Create the renderer const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer(); renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight); document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement); // Create a cube const geometry = new THREE.BoxGeometry(); const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0xffffff }); const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geometry, material); scene.add(cube); // Position the camera camera.position.z = 10; // Animate the cube function animate() { requestAnimationFrame(animate); cube.rotation.x += 0.005; cube.rotation.y += 0.005; renderer.render(scene, camera); } animate();
</script>
</body>
</html>

Let's make the cube a bit less cuber (more round)

Turns out, three js does not have a direct solution to this. So a quick google search yielded this neat little function (source)

function createBoxWithRoundedEdges( width, height, depth, radius0, smoothness ) { let shape = new THREE.Shape(); let eps = 0.00001; let radius = radius0 - eps; shape.absarc( eps, eps, eps, -Math.PI / 2, -Math.PI, true ); shape.absarc( eps, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI, Math.PI / 2, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI / 2, 0, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, eps, eps, 0, -Math.PI / 2, true ); let geometry = new THREE.ExtrudeBufferGeometry( shape, { amount: depth - radius0 * 2, bevelEnabled: true, bevelSegments: smoothness * 2, steps: 1, bevelSize: radius, bevelThickness: radius0, curveSegments: smoothness }); geometry.center(); return geometry;
}

Calling the function produces a neat little cube with rounded corners. you can tweak how much you would like to have the corners rounded with the 4th parameter - radius0. (don't forget to adjust smoothness as you make the radius larger. You'll see why once you do it)

const geometry = createBoxWithRoundedEdges( 1, 1, 1, .06, 20 ); //the smoother the better

Material + Lights

Now we want the cube to feel like the resend one - meaning we need to make it metal and a different light. Metal is easy - we already use standard material, so we just add some metalness and tone down roughness (while we are at it, we change the color to dark grey).

const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x2a2a2a, metalness: 1, roughness: 0.11 });

Now the cube is faded a lot - it almost blends into the background, and you occasionally see some flashes. We need to replace the light with a point light and tune it to always light some part of the cube up.

Compared to directional light, which gets emitted in a specific direction (but is quite weak and falling on a side of the cube creates a "flashlight" effect - you see small surface being affected), point light emits light in all directions, which creates a nice effect we can use - the light reflected in the cube will be larger, and always hitting surfaces - edges for even more cool effect. We create quite a large point light capable of lightning a whole side of the cube.

We can also add a second light for the undertones. Placing it below the cube ensures that we always have some part highlighted.

// Create the light
const light = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 100, 500 );
light.position.set( 0, 10, 10 );
scene.add( light ); const light2 = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 10, 500 );
light2.position.set( 0, -10, -5 );
scene.add( light2 );

You can play with the material properties and lights (intensities, colors) to create some cool effects.

Making baby cubes

Here comes the fun part. We will create another function which will call the rounded edges cube function in some nested loops and make 27 cubes. Let's go.

function makeCubes() { const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x1a1a1a, metalness: 1, roughness: 0.18 }); const numCubes = 3; // Create the group, we will add cubes to the group const cubes = new THREE.Group(); // iterate over all dimensions + trick to center the cube for (let i = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i++) { for (let j = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j++) { for (let k = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k++) { // adding the cubes const geom = createBoxWithRoundedEdges(1, 1, 1, .1, 20); geom.translate(i, j, k); const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geom, material); cubes.add(cube); } } } return cubes;
}

All we need to do now is call it:

// Create a cube
const cube = makeCubes();
scene.add(cube);

Adding controls

As a bonus we can add controls and rotate the cube as we like. If you want to disable the autorotation, just comment away the two cube.rotation lines.

First we need to import addons script. Place this line below the three import script.

<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three-addons@1.2.0/build/three-addons.min.js"></script>

Then we add the controls - you can add it anywhere, for example near the camera = ... line.

const controls = new THREE_ADDONS.OrbitControls(camera,renderer.domElement);

And finally we edit the animate function which gets called every time something is rendering.

// Animate the cube
function animate() { requestAnimationFrame(animate); cube.rotation.x += 0.005; cube.rotation.y -= 0.005; controls.update(); renderer.render(scene, camera);
}

Why does the resend cube look better?

  1. BLOOM - the glow effect you see coming off the cube is called bloom. In three js it is overly complicated to add (example_1 , example_2 ), and I was just too lazy. I have actually implemented it once in another project which I will perhaps share some other time.
  2. The mini cubes are not the same - resend has different geometry with the "squares" that are being extruded into cubes having rounded corners as well. Might be a good exercise for both you and me to try and make some other time.
  3. The effect of rotating layer - This looks cool and in theory could be made by always rotating just 1 layer - right before rotating, you can flip the cube by a before-defined, random transformation turning it on one of the sides. This way you can create the effect that you always rotate a different side.
  4. Better lightning - Nailing the lightning is difficult, so I approximated the best I could (I gave it 2 minutes).

I encourage you to add these features and tag me in the results - perhaps on Twitter. Have fun.

Final code:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head> <meta charset="UTF-8"> <title>Resend Cube</title> <style> body { margin: 0; } canvas { display: block; } </style>
</head>
<body> <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.142.0/build/three.min.js"></script>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three-addons@1.2.0/build/three-addons.min.js"></script> <script type="module"> function createBoxWithRoundedEdges( width, height, depth, radius0, smoothness ) { let shape = new THREE.Shape(); let eps = 0.00001; let radius = radius0 - eps; shape.absarc( eps, eps, eps, -Math.PI / 2, -Math.PI, true ); shape.absarc( eps, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI, Math.PI / 2, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, height - radius * 2, eps, Math.PI / 2, 0, true ); shape.absarc( width - radius * 2, eps, eps, 0, -Math.PI / 2, true ); let geometry = new THREE.ExtrudeBufferGeometry( shape, { depth: depth - radius0 * 2, bevelEnabled: true, bevelSegments: smoothness * 2, steps: 1, bevelSize: radius, bevelThickness: radius0, curveSegments: smoothness }); geometry.center(); return geometry; } function makeCubes() { const material = new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x2a2a2a, metalness: 1, roughness: 0.11 }); const numCubes = 3; // Create the group, we will add cubes to the group const cubes = new THREE.Group(); // iterate over all dimensions for (let i = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); i++) { for (let j = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); j++) { for (let k = -Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k <= Math.floor(numCubes / 2); k++) { // adding the cubes const geom = createBoxWithRoundedEdges(1, 1, 1, .17, 20); geom.translate(i, j, k); const cube = new THREE.Mesh(geom, material); cubes.add(cube); } } } return cubes; } // Create the scene const scene = new THREE.Scene(); scene.background = new THREE.Color( 0x000000 ); // Create the light const light = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 100, 500 ); light.position.set( 0, 5, 10 ); scene.add( light ); const light2 = new THREE.PointLight( 0xffffff, 10, 500 ); light2.position.set( 0, -10, -5 ); scene.add( light2 ); // Create the camera const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(75, window.innerWidth / window.innerHeight, 0.1, 1000); // Create the renderer const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer(); renderer.setSize(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight); document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement); // Create a cube const cube = makeCubes(); scene.add(cube); // Position the camera camera.position.z = 10; const controls = new THREE_ADDONS.OrbitControls(camera,renderer.domElement); // Animate the cube function animate() { requestAnimationFrame(animate); cube.rotation.x += 0.005; cube.rotation.y -= 0.005; controls.update(); renderer.render(scene, camera); } animate();
</script>
</body>
</html>

Manish Shambhoo Sah - Jun 16
